zh.lifeSummary: 當Václav II. König von Böhmen, Václav I. als König von Polen於1271年9月17日出生在海利格斯 洛米設斯 萊奇傑普拉哈時，他的父親Przemysl Otakar II King Of Bohmen39歲，母親Kunegunda Rostislavna26歲。他於1285年1月24日與Guta Juditha Gräfin von Habsburg在捷克伊赫拉瓦結婚。他們至少是3個兒子和5個女兒的父母。他去世於1305年6月21日在他的家鄉享年33歲，埋葬在捷克普拉哈。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Václav II. König von Böhmen, Václav I. als König von Polen于1271年9月17日出生在海利格斯 洛米設斯 萊奇傑普拉哈时，他的父亲Przemysl Otakar II King Of Bohmen39岁，母亲Kunegunda Rostislavna26岁。他于1285年1月24日与Guta Juditha Gräfin von Habsburg在捷克伊赫拉瓦结婚。他们至少是3个儿子和5个女儿的父母。他去世于1305年6月21日在他的家乡享年33岁，埋葬在捷克普拉哈。

nameMeanings.name: König
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Markus, Erna, Florian, Fritz, Gunther, Ilse, Manfred, Otto.  Jewish Chaim, Moishe, Moshe, Rozalia, Shmuel.

German and Jewish (Ashkenazic) (König): see Koenig ‘king’. The surname König is also found in some other European countries, e.g. in Czechia, Poland, Slovenia, and Croatia, where it was often applied as a translation into German of Slavic cognates like Czech Král (see Kral ) and Slovenian Kralj , and in the Netherlands (also in the form Konig) and Sweden.

variants.name: Koenig
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Otto, Hans, Erwin, Dieter, Frieda, Monika, Reinhard, Egon, Fritz, Heinz, Wilhelm.

German (mainly König) and Jewish (Ashkenazic; also König): from Middle High German künic, German König ‘king’, hence a German nickname for a servant or retainer of a king (for example, a farmer on a royal demesne), or alternatively a status name for the head of a craftmen's guild, or a society of sharpshooters or minstrels. As a Jewish surname, it is artificial, one of several such Ashkenazic names based on European titles of nobility or royalty. The surname Koenig is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine) and Poland. In eastern Germany the surname König is in part a Germanized form (translation into German) of Sorbian Kral ‘king’. Similarly, in Czechia, Poland, Slovenia, and Croatia, it was often applied as a translation into German of Czech, Polish, Slovenian, and Croatian cognates Král (see Kral ), Król (see Krol ), and Kralj , respectively. Compare Konig .

nameMeanings.name: von
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Altered form of Welsh   Vaughan  .
Chinese: variant of   Wong  .
Cambodian: written វ៉ុន, unexplained.

variants.name: Vaughan
meanings: Welsh: from fychan, a lenited form of bychan, a diminutive of bach ‘little’. This was often used to distinguish the younger of two bearers of the same personal name, typically the son of a father with the same name.

nameMeanings.name: Böhmen,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Fritz.  French Andre, Marthe.
Swiss German: from a short form of the medieval personal name Liborius (see Bohr 1 and Liborio ). This surname is also found in France (mainly Marne).
Swedish (Bohrén): ornamental name from Bohr 3 + the adjectival suffix -én, a derivative of Latin enius ‘relating to’.

variants.name: Bohr
meanings: German: from a short form of the medieval personal name Liborius (see Liborio ), the name of the patron saint of the city of Paderborn.
Germanized form of Sorbian Bór or other Slavic Bor .
Danish and Swedish: from North German   Bahr   ‘bear’.

nameMeanings.name: Václav
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Czech and Slovak (Václav): from the personal name Václav, Old Czech Vęceslav, Latinized as Wenceslaus, from an Old Slavic name composed of the elements vet'e ‘more, greater’ + slav ‘glory’ or ‘famous’ (from slava ‘glory’). It was borne by a 10th-century duke of Bohemia who fought against a revival of paganism in his territory; after his death he was adopted as patron saint of Bohemia.

variants.name: Vanek
meanings: Czech (Vaněk) and Slovak (also Vaněk): from a pet form of the personal name Václav (see Vaclav ). Compare Wanek and Wanke .

variants.name: Vaca
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Carlos, Juan, Francisco, Javier, Miguel, Salvador, Jesus, Luis, Manuel, Ramiro, Alberto.

Spanish and Portuguese: from the vocabulary word vaca ‘cow’ (from Latin vacca), probably applied as a metonymic occupational name for a cowherd or as a nickname for someone supposedly resembling a cow in some way.
Czech: from a pet form of the personal name Václav (see Vaclav ).

variants.name: Vana
meanings: Czech (Váňa): from the personal name Váňa, a derivative of Václav (see Vaclav ). This surname is also found in Slovakia.

variants.name: Vacek
meanings: Czech and Slovak: from a pet form of the personal name Václav (see Vaclav ).

variants.name: Vacha
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Czech Lubos, Bohumil.
Czech (Vácha): from a pet form of the personal name Václav (see Vaclav ).

variants.name: Vaclavik
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Czech and Slovak Milan.
Czech and Slovak (Václavík, Slovak also Václavik): from a pet form of the personal name Václav (see Vaclav ).

variants.name: Vacura
meanings: Czech: from a derivative of the personal name Václav (see Vaclav ).

variants.name: Vanecek
meanings: Czech (Vaněček): from a pet form of Váňa, a derivative of the personal name Václav (see Vaclav ).

variants.name: Vancura
meanings: Czech (Vančura): from a derivative of the personal name Václav (see Vaclav ).

variants.name: Vanicek
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Czech Miroslav, Lubomir.
Czech (Vaníček): from a pet form of the personal name Váňa, itself a pet form of Václav (see Vaclav ).

nameMeanings.name: als
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Jewish (eastern Ashkenazic): metonymic occupational name, nickname, or randomly assigned by Polish clerks, from Polish las ‘forest, wood’.
Jewish (American): shortened form of some original Ashkenazic surname.

nameMeanings.name: König
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Markus, Erna, Florian, Fritz, Gunther, Ilse, Manfred, Otto.  Jewish Chaim, Moishe, Moshe, Rozalia, Shmuel.

German and Jewish (Ashkenazic) (König): see Koenig ‘king’. The surname König is also found in some other European countries, e.g. in Czechia, Poland, Slovenia, and Croatia, where it was often applied as a translation into German of Slavic cognates like Czech Král (see Kral ) and Slovenian Kralj , and in the Netherlands (also in the form Konig) and Sweden.

variants.name: Koenig
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Otto, Hans, Erwin, Dieter, Frieda, Monika, Reinhard, Egon, Fritz, Heinz, Wilhelm.

German (mainly König) and Jewish (Ashkenazic; also König): from Middle High German künic, German König ‘king’, hence a German nickname for a servant or retainer of a king (for example, a farmer on a royal demesne), or alternatively a status name for the head of a craftmen's guild, or a society of sharpshooters or minstrels. As a Jewish surname, it is artificial, one of several such Ashkenazic names based on European titles of nobility or royalty. The surname Koenig is also found in France (Alsace and Lorraine) and Poland. In eastern Germany the surname König is in part a Germanized form (translation into German) of Sorbian Kral ‘king’. Similarly, in Czechia, Poland, Slovenia, and Croatia, it was often applied as a translation into German of Czech, Polish, Slovenian, and Croatian cognates Král (see Kral ), Król (see Krol ), and Kralj , respectively. Compare Konig .

nameMeanings.name: von
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Altered form of Welsh   Vaughan  .
Chinese: variant of   Wong  .
Cambodian: written វ៉ុន, unexplained.

variants.name: Vaughan
meanings: Welsh: from fychan, a lenited form of bychan, a diminutive of bach ‘little’. This was often used to distinguish the younger of two bearers of the same personal name, typically the son of a father with the same name.

nameMeanings.name: Polen
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: German and Dutch (Van Polen): ethnic name for a Pole or nickname for someone with some other connection with Poland, from Polen ‘Poland’.
Possibly also Irish: variant of   Polin  .

variants.name: Polin
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Hyman.
Irish (counties Armagh and Down): Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Póilín, a patronymic from a personal name formed from a pet form of Pól (see Paul ).
Jewish (eastern Ashkenazic): habitational name for someone from Poland, from a Slavicized form of German Polen ‘Poland’.

nameMeanings.name: Václav
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Czech and Slovak (Václav): from the personal name Václav, Old Czech Vęceslav, Latinized as Wenceslaus, from an Old Slavic name composed of the elements vet'e ‘more, greater’ + slav ‘glory’ or ‘famous’ (from slava ‘glory’). It was borne by a 10th-century duke of Bohemia who fought against a revival of paganism in his territory; after his death he was adopted as patron saint of Bohemia.

variants.name: Vanek
meanings: Czech (Vaněk) and Slovak (also Vaněk): from a pet form of the personal name Václav (see Vaclav ). Compare Wanek and Wanke .

variants.name: Vaca
meanings: Cornish forms of James.

variants.name: Vana
meanings: Mainly North American: of unknown origin, perhaps a transferred use of a surname that is fairly common in the United States. This may be of Irish origin, although the surname is not known in Ireland. Dana or Ana was the name of an ancient Irish fertility goddess, and this was also used in medieval times as a girl's name, However, it is not clear whether there is any connection between this name and the modern given name, which is sometimes also used as a feminine form of Dan or Daniel . Modern use as a boy's given name began in honour of Richard Henry Dana ( 1815–82 ), author of Two Years before the Mast, who supported the rights of fugitive slaves before and during the Civil War. The popularity of the given name was increased by the fame of the film star Dana Andrews ( 1909–92 ).

variants.name: Vacek
meanings: Czech and Slovak: from a pet form of the personal name Václav (see Vaclav ).

variants.name: Vacha
meanings: version of   Sasha .  This is one of the many names of Russian origin that were introduced to the English-speaking world, via French, at the time when  Diaghilev's  Ballet Russe made its great impact in Paris (  1909–20 ).

variants.name: Vaclavik
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Czech and Slovak Milan.
Czech and Slovak (Václavík, Slovak also Václavik): from a pet form of the personal name Václav (see Vaclav ).

variants.name: Vacura
meanings: Czech: from a derivative of the personal name Václav (see Vaclav ).

variants.name: Vanecek
meanings: Czech (Vaněček): from a pet form of Váňa, a derivative of the personal name Václav (see Vaclav ).

variants.name: Vancura
meanings: Czech (Vančura): from a derivative of the personal name Václav (see Vaclav ).

variants.name: Vanicek
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Czech Miroslav, Lubomir.
Czech (Vaníček): from a pet form of the personal name Váňa, itself a pet form of Václav (see Vaclav ).

nameMeanings.name: II.
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of   Violet  ,   Vivien  , or   Vivian .

variants.name: Vivien
meanings: Earlier generally taken as a variant of the boy's name Vivian , but now also rarely used for boys. This spelling was quite common in Old French. Its use as a girl's name in the English-speaking world was influenced by Tennyson's Merlin and Vivien ( 1859 ). This name, from Arthurian legend, may represent an altered form of a Celtic name (perhaps akin to the Irish Gaelic name Béibhinn ‘white lady’, pronounced ‘bee-veen’). The actress Vivien Leigh ( 1913–67 ) was christened Vivian.

variants.name: Violet
meanings: From the name of the flower (Old French violette, Late Latin violetta, a diminutive of viola). This was one of the earliest flower names to become popular in Britain, being used as early as 1700 and becoming well established in the 19th century, although it is now somewhat out of favour.

variants.name: Vivian
meanings: Originally a boy's name, from an Old French form of the Latin name Vivianus (probably a derivative of vivus ‘alive’), but now more frequent as a girl's name. The name was borne by a 5th-century bishop of Saintes in western France, remembered for protecting his people during the invasion of the Visigoths.

en.lifeSummary: When Václav II. König von Böhmen, Václav I. als König von Polen was born on 17 September 1271, in Prague, Bohemia, Holy Roman Empire, his father, Przemysl Otakar II King Of Bohmen, was 39 and his mother, Kunegunda Rostislavna, was 26. He married Guta Juditha Gräfin von Habsburg on 24 January 1285, in Jihlava, Czechia. They were the parents of at least 3 sons and 5 daughters. He died on 21 June 1305, in his hometown, at the age of 33, and was buried in Prague, Czechia.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Václav II. König von Böhmen, Václav I. als König von Polen est né le 17 septembre 1271, à Prague, Bohême, Saint-Empire romain, son père, Przemysl Otakar II King Of Bohmen, avait 39 ans et sa mère, Kunegunda Rostislavna, avait 26 ans. Il a épousé Guta Juditha Gräfin von Habsburg le 24 janvier 1285, à Jihlava, République tchèque. Ils ont eu au moins 3 fils et 5 filles. Il est décédé le 21 juin 1305, dans sa ville natale, à l’âge de 33 ans, et a été enterré à Prague, République tchèque.

de.lifeSummary: Als Václav II. König von Böhmen, Václav I. als König von Polen am 17. September 1271 in Prag, Böhmen, Heiliges Römisches Reich geboren wurde, war sein Vater Przemysl Otakar II King Of Bohmen 39 und seine Mutter Kunegunda Rostislavna 26. Er hat am 24. Januar 1285 in Jihlava, Tschechien Guta Juditha Gräfin von Habsburg geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 3 Söhne und 5 Töchter. Er starb am 21. Juni 1305 im Alter von 33 Jahren in seinem Geburtsort und wurde in Prag, Tschechien begraben.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Václav II. König von Böhmen, Václav I. als König von Polen è nato il il 17 settembre 1271 nel Praga, Čechy, Heiliges Römisches Reich, il suo padre, Przemysl Otakar II King Of Bohmen, aveva 39 anni e il suo madre, Kunegunda Rostislavna, aveva 26 anni. Lui sposato Guta Juditha Gräfin von Habsburg il 24 gennaio 1285, nel Jihlava, Cechia. Erano genitori di almeno 3 figli e 5 figlie. Morì il 21 giugno 1305, nel sua cittadina, all'età di 33 anni, e fu sepolto a Praga, Cechia.

ja.lifeSummary: Václav II. König von Böhmen, Václav I. als König von Polenが1271年9月17日にヘイリジェズ ルoミシェス レイチcエチイプラハで生まれた時，父Przemysl Otakar II King Of Bohmenは39歳，母Kunegunda Rostislavnaは26歳であった。1285年1月24日にチェコジhラヴァでGuta Juditha Gräfin von Habsburgと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも3人の息子と5人の娘の親であった。彼は1305年6月21日に故郷で33歳で死去し，チェコプラハにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Václav II. König von Böhmen, Václav I. als König von Polen(이)가 1271년 9월 17일 헤이리그스 o미슈스 라이치 cc히 프라하에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Przemysl Otakar II King Of Bohmen(은)는 39세였고, 그의 어머니 Kunegunda Rostislavna(은)는 26세였다. 그는 1285년 1월 24일 체코 j이흘라바에서 Guta Juditha Gräfin von Habsburg(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 세 명의 아들과 다섯 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1305년 6월 21일 그의 고향에서 33세의 나이로 사망했으며 체코 프라하에 묻혔다.

mn.17. September 1271–21. Juni 1305: 1271 оны Есдүгээр сарын 17–1305 оны Зургаадугаар сарын 21

pt.17. September 1271–21. Juni 1305: 17 de setembro de 1271–21 de junho de 1305
pt.lifeSummary: Quando Václav II. König von Böhmen, Václav I. als König von Polen nasceu em 17 de setembro de 1271, em Praha, Čechy, Heiliges Römisches Reich, seu pai, Przemysl Otakar II King Of Bohmen, tinha 39 anos e sua mãe, Kunegunda Rostislavna, tinha 26 anos. Ele casou-se com Guta Juditha Gräfin von Habsburg em 24 de janeiro de 1285, em Jihlava, Chequia. Eles tiveram pelo menos 3 filhos e 5 filhas. Ele faleceu em 21 de junho de 1305, em sua cidade natal, com 33 anos, e foi sepultado em Praha, Chequia.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Václav II. König von Böhmen, Václav I. als König von Polen родился 17 сентября 1271 г. в местности Прага, cечи, хилигесь рoмищесь раич, его отцу по имени Przemysl Otakar II King Of Bohmen было 39 лет, а матери, которую звали Kunegunda Rostislavna, 26. Он женился на женщине по имени Guta Juditha Gräfin von Habsburg 24 января 1285 г. в местности цзихлава, Чехия. У них было по крайней мере 3 сына и 5 дочерей. Он умер 21 июня 1305 г. в его родном городе в возрасте 33 лет и был похоронен в местности Прага, Чехия.

es.17. September 1271–21. Juni 1305: 17 de septiembre de 1271–21 de junio de 1305
es.lifeSummary: Cuando Václav II. König von Böhmen, Václav I. als König von Polen nació el 17 de septiembre de 1271, en Praga, Čechy, Heiliges Römisches Reich, su papá, Przemysl Otakar II King Of Bohmen, tenía 39 años y su mamá, Kunegunda Rostislavna, tenía 26 años. Se casó con Guta Juditha Gräfin von Habsburg el 24 de enero de 1285, en Jihlava, Chequia. Fueron padres de por lo menos 3 hijos y 5 hijas. Murió el 21 de junio de 1305, en su pueblo, a la edad de 33 años, y fue sepultado en Praga, Chequia.
